title: Quiz: Atomic Structure
description: Test your understanding of quantum mechanical atomic structure and electron configurations

questions.question: What is the maximum number of electrons that can occupy the n=3 shell?
questions.explanation: The maximum number of electrons in a shell is 2n². For n=3: 2(3)² = 18 electrons. This includes 3s (2), 3p (6), and 3d (10) orbitals.

questions.question: Which quantum number determines the shape of an atomic orbital?
questions.explanation: The angular momentum quantum number (l) determines orbital shape: l=0 gives spherical s orbitals, l=1 gives dumbbell-shaped p orbitals, l=2 gives cloverleaf d orbitals, etc.

questions.question: What is the electron configuration of iron (Fe, Z=26)?
questions.explanation: Iron has 26 electrons. Following the aufbau principle: 1s²(2) 2s²(4) 2p⁶(10) 3s²(12) 3p⁶(18) 4s²(20) 3d⁶(26). Note that 4s fills before 3d.

questions.question: According to Hund's rule, how do electrons fill the three 2p orbitals in nitrogen (N)?

options.text: Two electrons pair in one orbital, one electron in another
One electron in each orbital with parallel spins
All three electrons in the same orbital
One electron in each orbital with antiparallel spins

questions.explanation: Hund's rule states that electrons fill degenerate orbitals singly with parallel spins before pairing. Nitrogen's three 2p electrons each occupy a separate p orbital (px, py, pz) with the same spin orientation.

questions.question: The Pauli exclusion principle states that no two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers.
questions.explanation: True! The Pauli exclusion principle is fundamental to atomic structure. It's why each orbital can hold only 2 electrons (with opposite spins) and why atoms have the shell structure we observe.

questions.question: What is the energy of an electron in the n=2 shell of hydrogen?
questions.explanation: The energy of hydrogen energy levels is Eₙ = -13.6/n² eV. For n=2: E₂ = -13.6/4 = -3.4 eV.

questions.question: The hydrogen atom can be solved exactly using the Schrödinger equation because it's a two-body problem (one proton, one electron).
questions.explanation: True! The hydrogen atom is special because it involves only one electron-nucleus interaction. Multi-electron atoms involve electron-electron repulsion, making exact solutions impossible and necessitating approximation methods.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid quantum number combinations for an electron? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: a) Valid: 2p orbital with mₗ=0. b) Invalid: l must be < n, so l=1 is not allowed when n=1. c) Valid: 3d orbital with mₗ=-2. d) Invalid: for l=0 (s orbital), mₗ must equal 0, not 1.
